data.content_mdx:
The robot in your head — the one from movies — probably walks, talks, and has opinions about humanity. That robot doesn't really exist yet.

The ones that do exist are stranger. And honestly more interesting.

## Every robot does three things

No matter how simple or complex, every robot ever built follows the same loop:

**Sense** — it takes in information from the world. A camera. A microphone. A distance sensor. GPS coordinates.

**Think** — it processes that information and makes a decision. What does this data mean? What should I do next?

**Act** — it does something physical in the world. Moves a wheel. Opens a valve. Picks up an object.

This is the **Sense-Think-Act loop**. It's the skeleton of every robot ever built — from a ₹2,000 toy to a Mars rover.

## Three robots. Same loop.

**A Roomba**
- Sense: bump and cliff sensors
- Think: "I hit something — turn right."
- Act: wheels reverse, robot turns

**A factory robotic arm**
- Sense: position encoders at each joint
- Think: follow the pre-programmed path
- Act: motors move with millimetre precision

**A self-driving car**
- Sense: cameras, radar, LiDAR
- Think: neural networks + path planning
- Act: steers, brakes, accelerates

The loop is identical. The "Think" step is what varies — from a few lines of if-else to deep neural networks.

## Why this matters

Once you see the loop, you see it everywhere. Thermostats. Autopilots. Recommendation algorithms. They all follow Sense → Think → Act.

It also tells you exactly where robots fail: it sensed wrong, it thought wrong, or it acted wrong. That's the whole list.

data.question: A motion-sensor light turns ON when you walk past. Which part of the loop is the motion sensor?

options.explanation: Exactly. Motion detection is information about the world coming in.
Detection is taking input; deciding what to do with it is the Think step.

options.text: Act — it turns the light on
options.explanation: Turning on the light is Act. The sensor itself only senses.
It does fit — Sense covers any input from the world.

cards.back: Taking information in from the world (cameras, distance, GPS…)
Processing input + deciding what to do next
Doing something physical — moving, gripping, signalling
The skeleton of every robot ever built
